data.title: Trump vs EU: the European laws curbing big tech and irking US president
data.description:
The European Union is back in the crosshairs of the Trump administration over its tech rules, which Washington denounced as an attempt to “coerce” American social media platforms into censoring viewpoints they oppose.
The US State Department said on Tuesday it would deny visas to a former EU commissioner and four others, saying they “have advanced censorship crackdowns by foreign states – in each case targeting American speakers and American companies”.
Trump has vowed to punish countries that seek to curb US big tech firms.
Brussels has adopted a powerful legal arsenal aimed at reining in tech giants – namely through its Digital Markets Act (DMA), which covers competition, and the Digital Services Act (DSA) on content moderation.
The EU has already slapped heavy fines on US behemoths including Apple, Meta and X under the new rules.

Digital Services Act
Rolled out in stages since 2023, the mammoth Digital Services Act forces online firms to aggressively police content in the 27 countries of the European Union or face major fines.
Aimed at protecting consumers from disinformation and hate speech as well as counterfeit or dangerous goods, it obliges platforms to swiftly remove illegal content or make it inaccessible.
The law instructs platforms to suspend users who frequently share illegal content such as hate speech – a provision framed as “censorship” by detractors across the Atlantic.
Tougher rules apply to a designated list of “very large” platforms that include US giants Apple, Amazon, Facebook, Google, Instagram, Microsoft, Snapchat and X.
These giants must assess dangers linked to their services regarding illegal content and privacy, set up internal risk mitigation systems, and give regulators access to their data to verify compliance.
Violators can face fines of up to six per cent of global turnover, and the EU has the power to ban offending platforms from Europe for repeated non-compliance.
Elon Musk’s X was hit with the first fine under the DSA on December 5, a €120 million (US$140 million) penalty for a lack of transparency over what the EU calls the deceptive design of its “blue checkmark” for supposedly verified accounts, and its failure to provide access to public data for researchers.
Digital Markets Act
Since March 2024, the world’s biggest digital companies have faced strict EU rules intended to limit abuses linked to market dominance, favour the emergence of start-ups in Europe and improve options for consumers.
Brussels has so far named seven so-called gatekeepers covered by the Digital Markets Act: Google’s Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, TikTok parent ByteDance, Facebook and Instagram parent Meta, Microsoft and travel giant Booking.
Gatekeepers can be fined for locking in customers to use pre-installed services, such as a web browser, mapping or weather information.
The DMA has forced Google to overhaul its search display to avoid favouring its own services – such as Google Flights or shopping.
It requires that users be able to choose where they download apps – without going via the dominant two players, Apple’s App Store and Google Play.
And it has forced Apple to allow developers to offer alternative payment options directly to consumers – outside the App Store, hitting it with a fine of €500 million (US$589 million) in April.
The DMA has also imposed interoperability between messaging apps WhatsApp and Messenger and competitors who request it.
The EU fined Meta €200 million (US$236 million) in April over its “pay or consent” system after it violated rules on the use of personal data on Facebook and Instagram.
Failure to comply with the DMA can carry fines in the billions of dollars, reaching 20 per cent of global turnover for repeat offenders.

GPDR and AI
The EU’s data protection rules (GDPR) have also tripped up US tech giants, with Brussels issuing numerous fines since they came into force in 2018.
The rules require firms to seek the consent of users to collect personal data and to explain what it will be used for, and give users the right to ask firms to delete personal data.
Fines for violations can go as high as €20 million (US$23.6 million) or four per cent of a company’s global turnover.
The EU has also adopted its AI act which will gradually bring in guardrails on using artificial intelligence in high-risk areas such as security, health and civic rights. In the face of pressure from the industry, the EU is considering weakening the measures and delaying their implementation.

data.title: Hong Kong police make another arrest over deadly Tai Po blaze
data.description:
Hong Kong police have made their 16th arrest in connection with last month’s deadly Tai Po blaze, detaining a man on suspicion of manslaughter.
Police earlier arrested 15 people in connection with the fire, which engulfed seven of eight scaffolding-clad residential buildings in the Wang Fuk Court housing estate on November 26, killing 161 and displacing almost 5,000 residents.
Police said on Wednesday that they arrested a man, 48, in Tin Shui Wai. He was the person in charge of the external wall repair subcontractor at the estate, which was undergoing renovations when the blaze broke out. He was later released on bail.




An investigation by the New Territories North regional crime unit is continuing.
Separately, the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (Hong Kong) said on Wednesday that DNA testing by authorities had identified the remains of a cat and a dog from Wang Fuk Court. The remains were transferred to the SPCA, which has contacted the pet owners to retrieve them.
At least 151 animals were killed in the fire. The NGO said it would maintain close contact with police and support the families affected.

data.title: Algeria brands French colonisation a crime, demands apology, reparations in landmark move
data.description:
Algeria’s parliament unanimously approved on Wednesday a law declaring France’s colonisation of the country a crime, and demanding an apology and reparations.
Standing in the chamber, lawmakers wearing scarves in the colours of the national flag chanted “long live Algeria” as they applauded the passage of the bill, which said France held “legal responsibility for its colonial past in Algeria and the tragedies it caused”.
The vote comes as the two countries are embroiled in a major diplomatic crisis, and analysts say that while Algeria’s move is largely symbolic, it is still politically significant.
Parliament speaker Brahim Boughali told the APS state news agency before the vote that it would send “a clear message, both internally and externally, that Algeria’s national memory is neither erasable nor negotiable”.
The legislation listed the “crimes of French colonisation”, including nuclear tests, extrajudicial killings, “physical and psychological torture”, and the “systematic plundering of resources”.

It said that “full and fair compensation for all material and moral damages caused by French colonisation is an inalienable right of the Algerian state and people”.
France’s rule over Algeria from 1830 until 1962 remains a sore spot in relations between the two countries.
The period was marked by mass killings and large-scale deportations, all the way up to the bloody war of independence from 1954 to 1962.
Algeria says the war killed 1.5 million people, while French historians put the death toll lower at 500,000 in total, 400,000 of them Algerian.
French President Emmanuel Macron had previously acknowledged the colonisation of Algeria was a “crime against humanity”, but has stopped short of offering an apology.
Asked last week about the vote, French foreign ministry spokesman Pascal Confavreux said he would not comment on “political debates taking place in foreign countries”.
Hosni Kitouni, a researcher in colonial history at the University of Exeter in the UK, said that “legally, this law has no international scope and therefore is not binding for France”.
But “its political and symbolic significance is important: it marks a rupture in the relationship with France in terms of memory,” he said.

data.title: Christmas Eve crowds gather in Hong Kong but festive atmosphere ‘more subdued’
data.description:
Hongkongers are out in droves to experience festive vibes on Christmas Eve, while more than 330,000 residents have left the city for the holidays.
By Wednesday evening, a growing crowd had gathered at the open-air plaza at Harbour City shopping centre in Tsim Sha Tsui to admire special Disney-themed festive installations.
Office worker Cecile Tang, 38, was there with her mother, her husband and their two children. The five viewed the Christmas decorations at Harbour City and took a stroll along the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront before heading home for a family dinner.
Tang said the atmosphere appeared to be less festive this year. “It’s not as lively as before, especially in Kowloon where there seem to be fewer Christmas decorations this year,” she said, adding that many of her friends had left the city for the holidays.
With a budget of HK$1,000 (US$128) to HK$2,000 for celebrations, the family opted for a home-cooked meal.
“It’s more comfortable at home, and restaurants feel a bit overpriced,” Tang said.

A member of the city’s disciplined services, surnamed Fung, also pointed to the less vibrant festive atmosphere as he spent the evening at the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront with his family of four.
“Perhaps due to the Tai Po fire, fewer people are out celebrating,” he said, referring to the Wang Fuk Court inferno last month that claimed 161 lives and left thousands homeless.
The family budgeted around HK$1,000 for their celebrations, covering a restaurant meal and small gifts like lipstick and clothes for their two daughters.
As of 4pm, 333,598 residents had left Hong Kong, while 225,249 people, including returning Hongkongers, had entered the city. Among the arrivals, 36 per cent were mainland Chinese tourists.
Some mainlanders said they were visiting Hong Kong to experience the festive atmosphere as Christmas was not a public holiday across the border.
Wang, a Shenzhen resident in her thirties, posed for photos with four friends along the Victoria Harbour waterfront, all of whom were dressed in festive Christmas attire.

Wang said the biggest difference between Christmas in Hong Kong and on the mainland was the sheer number of people. “Although it’s crowded, we come for Christmas every year,” she said. “Because there are more Christmas activities here.”
A 68-year-old retiree from Beijing, who identified herself as Liu, was among a 20-strong tour group in Hong Kong for a one-night stay.
“On the mainland, not so many people celebrate Christmas, and we seniors rarely take part,” she said.
“Everyone seems to be participating and placing importance on it here. After all, it’s a Western holiday, and Hong Kong residents have been influenced by the culture, so they’re more keen to join in.”

Crowds also gathered at the West Kowloon Cultural District to celebrate the festivities. Its Christmas Town attraction has more than 60 trees, including a 12-metre-tall centrepiece, artificial snow experiences and meet-and-greets with Santa.
Marketing worker Quincy Cheng Mei-ting, 34, opted to stay in Hong Kong with her husband and three-year-old son for Christmas, splurging HK$2,500 for a buffet in Mong Kok after taking pictures at West Kowloon.
“This year, my son is much more verbal and constantly points out every Santa he sees, so I took him out today to take it all in. Even though it’s quite crowded, it feels just like I remember it as a kid heading to Tsim Sha Tsui for the Christmas lights,” Cheng said.
“As for the buffet, it’s a way to reward ourselves for the constant juggle of balancing our careers and raising a child. It really feels like we made it.”
Samme Cheng Pak-man, vice-chairman of catering trade body the Institute of Dining Professionals, said he expected the restaurant industry to enjoy a 10 per cent increase in business over the Christmas holiday compared with last year.
“Reservations are more robust this year. Foot traffic is noticeably higher in bustling districts like Mong Kok, Causeway Bay and Tsim Sha Tsui, which provides a bigger boost for restaurants there,” Cheng said.
He added that mid-range Christmas sets priced between HK$500 and HK$800 per person were the most popular, with locals mostly being the patrons.
“After all, the spending patterns of mainland tourists have shifted, and there is a gap in value for money between Hong Kong’s dining sector and the mainland’s,” he said.
Rail giant the MTR will provide an overnight service on all lines on Christmas Eve, except the Airport Express, Disneyland Resort line and journeys to or from Lo Wu and Lok Ma Chau stations on the East Rail line. Special arrangements will be extended to the Airport Express, with the last train from AsiaWorld-Expo leaving at 1.30am.
For franchised bus services, Citybus will add special route 260X between Central and Stanley, while KMB will operate route W4 connecting West Kowloon and Tsim Sha Tsui.

data.title: Who are the NBA’s ‘most viewed’ players, and where does rookie Cooper Flagg rank?
data.description:
What a difference a year makes. On Tuesday, in preparation for its busy slate of Christmas Day games, the NBA released a top-10 list of the most viewed players across all of its social media platforms this season.
The league did this last year, too. At the time, Dallas Mavericks star player Luka Doncic did not appear in the top 10.
Fast forward to this season, and now he is on top of it.
Doncic is not only the most-viewed player in the NBA after his stunning midseason trade from the Mavs to the LA Lakers, he also has quite a bit of cushion between himself and second place.
According to NBA Communications, Doncic clips and highlights account for 845 million views across their platforms so far this season. At No 2 is Spurs centre Victor Wembanyama at 708 million. LeBron James, who topped the list in 2024, comes in at No 4, with a number (641 million) more than 200 million fewer than Doncic.
It points to the argument that Doncic, who has carried the NBA’s most popular franchise to a 19-8 record, is now the most popular player in the league.

That might sting for Mavericks fans, but it is not all bad: there is only one rookie in the top 10 most viewed list, and he plays in Dallas.
Mavericks No 1 overall draft pick Cooper Flagg cracked the list at No 9, ahead of Bucks star Giannis Antetokounmpo. Flagg's highlights have garnered 312 million views so far during his electric rookie season. Not only is Flagg the only rookie on this year’s list, but there were none on last year’s list either.
Nine of the list of 10 players will be in action on Christmas, including Flagg and the Mavericks. They are set to take on the Golden State Warriors and Steph Curry, who came in at No 3 (703 million views).
Top 10 most-viewed players in the NBA

Luka Doncic, Los Angeles Lakers, 845 million


Victor Wembanyama, San Antonio Spurs, 708 million


Steph Curry, Golden State Warriors, 703 million


LeBron James, Los Angeles Lakers, 641 million


Shai Gilgeous-Alexander, Oklahoma City Thunder, 496 million


Nikola Jokic, Denver Nuggets, 445 million


Austin Reaves, Los Angeles Lakers, 409 million


Kevin Durant, Houston Rockets, 331 million


Cooper Flagg, Dallas Mavericks, 312 million


Giannis Antetokounmpo, Milwaukee Bucks, 304 million

data.title: Chinese AI champion iFlytek sets up new entity to expand into semiconductor design
data.description:
US-blacklisted company iFlytek has set up a new corporate entity to expand into semiconductors and other artificial intelligence-related fields as part of efforts to bolster China’s broader tech self-sufficiency initiatives.
The new entity, Shandong Yixun Information Technology, was incorporated last week with a registered capital of 50 million yuan (US$7.1 million), according to the latest information from Chinese corporate data provider Qichacha.
The scope of this entity’s operations would include integrated circuit design, AI software development and technical consulting for AI public services online platforms, according to Qichacha.
Based in Hefei, the capital of eastern Anhui province, iFlytek on Wednesday said it did not have additional information about the business of Shandong Yixun.
The latest move by iFlytek, which made its name in the area of voice-recognition software, underscored the growing importance of semiconductor design in China, where a growing number of domestic tech companies are developing AI processors that could potentially rival those from industry heavyweights Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices.
It comes amid this week’s determination by the US Trade Representative Office that Beijing’s drive for dominance in semiconductors was “unreasonable and discriminatory” and posed a direct threat to US commerce.
The agency said the US was implementing a tariff action on a wide range of Chinese semiconductors, with an initial rate of 0 per cent that would rise to a higher level in 18 months on June 23, 2027. The tariff rate would be announced 30 days before the deadline.

Washington added iFlytek to the US government’s Entity List in 2019, which barred the Chinese company from obtaining US chips without a licence.
The company has been among the most vocal advocates for exclusively using locally designed chips for AI development projects.
In June, chairman and founder Liu Qingfeng said iFlytek was likely “the only Chinese AI model developer that insisted on using domestic chips”, including the Ascend 910B developed by Huawei Technologies.
Liu admitted, however, that using mainland-designed semiconductors could delay the development of AI models by three months, but insisted that iFlytek would continue to use these chips and push improved processes.
The company had earlier claimed that its X-series AI models, with the latest version X1.5 released in November, were China’s only mainstream model family trained entirely with domestic computing power.
While China has significantly closed the gap with the US in AI development, it remains years behind in cutting-edge semiconductors, according to analysts at investment group Citic CLSA.

data.title: The write way: Hong Kong’s Christmas card loyalists keep the digital wave at bay
data.description:
Amber Lam has been writing and making her own Christmas cards since childhood and enjoys handing them personally to friends each festive season.
The 25-year-old clerk, who sends about 20 cards a year, started the tradition when she was in primary school.
She says handwritten messages help build personal bonds with loved ones.
“I’ve always loved giving handwritten cards, but normally there aren’t many chances to just sit down and write letters to people. Christmas feels like the perfect opportunity,” Lam said.
Despite the ease of sending mass messages online or via social media, diehard fans still insist on delivering their festive greetings the traditional way, through paper cards, before Christmas each year.
“I don’t like e‑cards and WhatsApp just doesn’t have any warmth,” Lam explained.
For her, the cards serve as both a year-end reflection and gesture of love – a way to share her updates while also supporting friends with whatever they are facing.
Commercial printer Fingerprint has seen stable demand for physical cards despite the digital shift, according to director William Ho Leong-tung, who said that some individuals and institutions still prefer ordering personalised cards.
Corporate customers accounted for about 80 per cent of orders, with individuals making up the rest.
Some companies order up to 2,000 cards each year, he said.
But business from institutions has fallen. “The corporate side dropped this year by about 20 per cent,” he said, attributing the decline to the growing dominance of electronic greetings.
“People who are still sending Christmas cards these days have something they want to hold on to. The personal touch from a printed card is still important.”
He contrasted this intimacy with the ease of text messages: “A WhatsApp message can be broadcast to a thousand friends, and then you may reply with a simple ‘Merry Christmas.’ That doesn’t carry much of a personal touch.”
Ho said fans of printed cards span all age groups, depending on whether the designs appeal to their tastes.
He prints several thousand paper cards each year, and the numbers have remained stable. However, production has been more costly, with paper prices rising about 10 per cent compared with last year.

Gifting & Design Studio founder Krystal Tolani said the markets for paper cards and e-cards rarely overlap.
Some 25 to 35 per cent of the shop’s annual revenue came from Christmas card sales.
Tolani said customers did not want generic cards or artificial intelligence-generated art but something niche and Hong Kong-specific.
“Paper cards demonstrate an effort made – from browsing shelves, handwriting a message, to delivering it in person or via post. It can’t be replicated digitally,” she said.
“My e-cards received are somewhere in my inbox, likely to never be opened again.”
Graphics designer Kate Wong said the greeting card market was currently dominated by e-cards, mainly due to rising environmental awareness and the widespread use of social media and messaging apps, which offer convenience and instant delivery.
Those who still choose physical cards often value emotional warmth, so they tend to select cards with texture and quality such as cotton paper, she said.
For those receiving or sending e-cards, Patrizia Yeung, founder and chief consultant at YiLi International Etiquette, recommends replying to greetings within 48 hours and personalising messages beyond just a name.
She also cautioned against forwarding an e-card sent by others.

data.title: China, Russia clash with US at UN, scientists on ‘benefits of lard’: SCMP daily highlights
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China stories of the day. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. China, Russia clash with US at UN over Venezuela oil seizures and naval pressure
China accused the United States at the United Nations of violating international law and destabilising the Caribbean region, as Washington defended its seizure of Venezuelan oil tankers and vowed to intensify pressure on President Nicolas Maduro’s government.
2. Beijing is pinning hopes for Taiwan reunification on KMT – but not all of them
The party’s new chairwoman Cheng Li-wun has adopted a mainland-friendly tone but it remains to be seen if she can live up to Beijing’s hopes.
3. China needs ‘original’ knowledge system on border history to counter West, scholar says

China should establish an “original” knowledge system regarding the history of its border regions to mitigate security risks from “hostile forces in the West”, according to a senior scholar at a prominent state-run think tank.
4. China’s Xi urges state giants to be pillars of national development
President Xi Jinping has instructed central state-owned enterprises (SOEs) to serve as the bedrock of national development, urging them to drive industrial upgrading and secure critical technologies in the next phase of China’s growth.
5. Taipei mayor cuts Shanghai visit short to oversee security following stabbing
Originally scheduled to attend the Shanghai-Taipei City Forum on Saturday and Sunday, Chiang will now only take part in the main forum on Sunday morning before returning to Taipei to oversee heightened security operations, according to a press release from the municipal government on Tuesday.
6. Will US-led ‘Pax Silica’ be a new dawn for the international AI supply chain?
In its push to secure supplies of critical minerals, the Donald Trump administration has increasingly looked abroad to reduce vulnerabilities in supply chains that underpin many of the world’s most advanced technologies.
7. Lard can actually keep you fit and feeling more comfortable, Chinese study finds

Consuming a diet with a moderate amount of lard can decrease both fat accumulation and inflammation in body fat, according to a new study by Chinese scientists.

data.title: Hard work and long hours fuelled China’s rise. Now, it mulls the benefits of longer breaks
data.description:
Whether in China’s urban centres or its remote rural areas, Christmas is not typically celebrated with the fanfare seen in the West. On the mainland, December 25 is not even a public holiday, with the lion’s share of enthusiasm reserved for the traditional Lunar New Year period beginning in January or February.
Zhang Li, who lives in a pastoral section of the Inner Mongolia autonomous region in the country’s north, was no exception. But this year things have changed, thanks in part to a new government policy – her daughter has a “snow break” from her middle school coinciding with the Christmas season, and her boss has suggested she also take the time off.
From December 24 to 26, as well as the following weekend, all school students under grade nine in Hulunbuir will have a five-day break. A recent government directive encouraged the city’s youth to go outdoors and take part in the city’s ice and snow tourism festival.
“My department head said that regardless of whether we have kids or not, as long as there’s nothing urgent at hand, everyone can take these days off,” said Zhang, who works at a local government office.
As one of the hardest-working nations in the world, Chinese people are now being urged to take more leisure time. The call to relax carries another purpose – longer breaks are an incentive for the country’s consumers to spend more.
While some countries are floating plans to cut public holidays amid fiscal pressure and concerns over productivity, China – whose work weeks are among the world’s longest – is examining the benefits of more time off.
Longer leave entitlements mean more people are getting outdoors and injecting money into the slowing economy, hastening an economic transition that would make household spending the main driver of future growth instead of investment and exports, analysts said.
From Zhang’s perspective, the newly created holiday in Hulunbuir serves this purpose well.
“The summer grassland scenery here brings substantial tourism revenue, but this has declined since the coronavirus pandemic, so we’re trying to find growth from winter tourism as well – starting by encouraging short-distance journeys among local residents,” she explained.


Like many local families, Zhang and her daughter plan to spend part of the holiday at the city’s newly built ice and snow amusement park. The rest will be devoted to an educational study tour in a nearby city.
On top of public holidays, she normally has seven days of paid leave each year, with the “snow break” coming as a bonus.
Across China, employees log an average of 48.5 hours per week, according to data from the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) – roughly 2,400 hours per year, significantly more than any member country of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development.
Chinese authorities are “supporting regions with suitable conditions to introduce spring and autumn school holidays, while making sure workers’ paid leave is properly staggered and fully honoured”, according to a statement issued after the central economic work conference earlier this month, an annual meeting which sets the tone for economic policy in the coming year.
Several local governments appear to be following these instructions. Sichuan and Zhejiang provinces are giving students extra days off in the spring and autumn, while parts of Jilin province and the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region have, like Hulunbuir, introduced “snow breaks”.
Inadequate leisure time has previously been cited by economists as one reason for insufficient consumer demand, a difficulty Chinese policymakers have attempted to surmount through experimentation.
“To some extent, the long working hours of Chinese employees restrict their spending on service consumption,” said Luo Zhiheng, chief economist at Yuekai Securities, at a forum in Beijing last week.
China’s retail sales, a major gauge of consumption, grew 1.3 per cent year on year in November, the metric’s weakest pace in almost three years, NBS figures showed. Boosting domestic demand should be a top priority in 2026, officials were told at the central economic work conference.


Luo suggested the government add new public holidays in the second half of the year, on top of traditional festivals. “Increasing holidays would reduce production and supply, but it would at the same time boost consumer spending, leading to a better supply-demand balance for China,” he said.
But the story is different in places like Europe, where social welfare benefits are relatively high and working hours are short, a model viewed by some policymakers as one that sacrifices potential economic growth.
Some countries are attempting to change that calculus. Earlier this year, Slovakia cut one public holiday in a move officials said would strengthen the country’s fiscal position, while France also attempted to eliminate two holidays on the grounds of easing budgetary strains and improving economic activity. The latter measure failed after intense public backlash.
For China, adding seasonal school holidays is a relatively simple move, some researchers said – but whether parents receive the leave to match remains a challenge in the current work culture.
“Amid economic headwinds, job stability often outweighs paid holidays for most average workers – and in the intense ‘996’ culture that dominates China, it’s tough for employees to even request their entitled leave,” said Liu Simin, a council member at the Beijing Tourism Society.
The ‘996’ phenomenon, where employees in some industries feel pressured to work long hours, received its name after workers complained online of shifts running from 9am to 9pm six days a week.
Creating extended holidays by reshuffling workdays, despite drawing some criticism in recent years, remains a more practical and feasible alternative to boost leisure time without straining workplace dynamics, he added.

data.title: AI-for-Science start-up DP Technology raises US$114 million in Series C round
data.description:
DP Technology – the developer of a suite of AI tools used by researchers for work spanning drug design to battery development – has raised more than 800 million yuan (US$114 million) in Series C financing to expand research and development (R&D) and hire talent.
The Beijing-based AI-for-Science (AI4S) start-up said on Wednesday that the round drew backing from a mix of state-linked and venture investors, including Fortune Venture Capital, Beijing Jingguorui Equity Investment Fund, Beijing Artificial Intelligence Industry Investment Fund, Beijing Pharmaceutical and Health Industry Investment Fund, Lenovo Capital and Incubator Group and Oriza Hua.
The funding would be used primarily to recruit talent and expand R&D, the company said.
Founded in 2018 by two Peking University alumni, DP Technology focuses on developing AI4S tools for fundamental research, life sciences and materials science. The sector uses artificial intelligence and advanced computing to accelerate scientific discovery, from molecular simulation to laboratory automation.
DP Technology’s product line-up spans multiple stages of the research process.
Its offerings include Particle Universe, an AI model system for scientific simulation; Bohrium, a cloud-based research platform and asset hub; and Uni-Lab, a laboratory operating system.
The company has also developed Hermite, a computer-aided drug design platform; RiDYMO, an R&D platform targeting previously “undruggable” biological targets; Piloteye, a battery design platform; Lebesgue Intelligent Computing, a computing traffic controller; and SciMaster, a general-purpose scientific AI agent that integrates with its other products.
The company said its platforms served more than 3 million users across 1,000 universities and research organisations worldwide, as well as more than 150 corporate clients, including PetroChina, Contemporary Amperex Technology Limited and BYD.
“AI for Science is more than just an emerging sector – it is a foundational infrastructure project for decades of scientific discovery to come,” Zhang Linfeng, founder and chief scientist of DP Technology, said in a statement.
Zhang was part of the team that won the 2020 ACM Gordon Bell Prize – often referred to as the “Nobel Prize of supercomputing” – for research into machine-learning methods that achieved record-breaking molecular dynamics simulations.
The company’s long-term ambition was to build “AI scientists” and an “intelligent system for scientific discovery”, according to Sun Weijie, founder and chief executive of DP Technology.
DP Technology previously completed a Series B financing round in January 2022, which raised tens of millions of dollars in a deal co-led by Source Code Capital and Qiming Venture Partners, with continued participation from GL Ventures and Matrix Partners China.
Earlier rounds included a Series A led by GL Ventures with Matrix Partners China and BioMap as co-investors, following a pre-A round led by Vision Plus Capital with Crystal Stream as a co-investor and an angel round backed by Baidu Venture, Will Hunting Capital and Peakview Capital.
The deal comes as competition to lead AI4S development accelerates globally.
In August, China’s State Council listed “AI Plus Scientific Research” as part of its national “AI Plus” action plan.
In November, the United States launched the “Genesis Mission”, a federal initiative aimed at supporting artificial intelligence research and its scientific applications.
